comments.text: I wrote a short note showing that the asymptotic currently stated on this page cannot be correct. In the note I give an explicit construction proving\[g_3(n)\ge \frac{n\log\log n}{\log n} + (M+1+o(1))\frac{n}{\log n},\]where \(M\) is the Meissel--Mertens constant. In particular, this rules out any conjectural asymptotic of the form \(g_3(n)=\frac{n\log\log n}{\log n} + (c+o(1))\frac{n}{(\log n)^2}.\) Motivated by this, a natural revised question is whether there exists an absolute constant $c>0$ such that, for all sufficiently large $n$,\[ g_3(n)\le \frac{n\log\log n}{\log n}+c\frac{n}{\log n}. \]Finally, as discussed in Section 3 of the note, Erdős' bound in [Er64d] yields\[ g_3(n)\le \frac{n\log\log n}{\log n}+O\!\left(\frac{n}{(\log n)^{1+\delta}}\right) \]for some $\delta>0$.

Aha, thanks! In fact this construction is very similar to that given by Erdős in [Er64d] when he proved the lower bound of the given asymptotic. That was just taking the set of all products of two primes $p<q$ with\[\frac{n}{\log n}<pq<n\]and\[(\log n)^2 < p < q^{1/4}.\]The number of such pairs Erdős records as\[(1+o(1))\frac{n\log\log n}{\log n}\]but, as you note, it's a classical fact that in fact it's equal to\[\frac{n\log\log n}{\log n}+(M+o(1))\frac{n}{\log n}.\]Your construction is basically this (albeit a little simpler, taking advantage of fixing $k=3$), together with the set of all primes as well. But even just taking the product of two primes already gives a second order term $\gg \frac{n}{\log n}$. This makes the denominator of $(\log n)^2$ in the problem here strange, since Erdős' construction already disproved it. Perhaps this was a typo in [Er69]? Or maybe he'd miscalculated the asymptotics. Also, there is something strange in that this two-primes construction lower bound also contradicts the\[g_3(n)\leq \frac{n\log\log n}{\log n}+O\left(\frac{n}{(\log n)^{1+\delta}}\right)\]upper bound you mention here. So is there also an error in this upper bound? EDIT: Ah, yes, Erdős' proof actually gives an upper bound of\[g_3(n) \leq \frac{n\log\log n}{\log n}+O\left(\frac{n}{\log n}\right),\]so he's wrong on the final page where he says the bound you mention. In Erdős' upper bound proof he early on discards those integers which may themselves be primes (which of course may contribute $\asymp \frac{n}{\log n}$ to the count). I think that his bound of $\frac{n}{(\log n)^{1+\delta}}$ is only for the error term after some initial pruning of $A$ (e.g. discarding elements of $A$ which are prime or a prime multiplied by a small integer). Looking at Erdős' upper bound proof I do wonder if just sharpening this up a bit would already give an asymptotic for the second order term.

On reading page 80 of [Er69] I think the second part of the question is only for $k=3$ (and your formulation is somewhat implicit; Erdos simply writes "it is not clear whether (the bound he could prove) can be sharpened"). (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Thanks - the second part having $g_k$ rather than a $g_3$ is definitely just a typo on my part (indeed it's contradicted by the general asymptotic in the remarks!) I agree that the question as I have written is implicit; I was trying to avoid simply stating Erdős' bounds and then asking 'can this be improved?'. Indeed, to be pedantic, Erdős never explicitly asked this or posed this as a problem; I just liked the result and wanted to include it on the site as a signpost to an area Erdős had thought about that still had some uncertainty (and would argue that he did implicitly pose this, or at least the case $k=3$, as a problem).
